title: ‘Venom: Let There Be Carnage’ and Top 5 Comic Book Movie Sequels
format: Sean Fennessy talks 'Venom: Let There Be Carnage' with Van Lathan and Charles Holmes, the Midnight Boys, then the three of them count down their top five comic book movie sequels. Plus: Sean's interview with director Andy Serkis.

segments.heading: ‘Venom: Let There Be Carnage’
segments.note: With Van Lathan and Charles Holmes (the Midnight Boys)

films.title: Venom: Let There Be Carnage
films.note: Charles loved it without irony, calling it a rom-com about Eddie and Venom as a bickering old married couple. Van agrees it's a terrible movie by any normal metric but says he was never bored. Sean points to the 90-minute runtime, the low stakes (San Francisco, not the world), and a cast with nine combined Oscar nominations as evidence the movie knows exactly what it is.

films.title: Venom
films.note: The surprise hit this is a sequel to. Andy Serkis notes Tom Hardy's performance there was mostly internalized, a 'radio play' with Venom rarely onscreen; the sequel has him manifest fully.

films.title: Marriage Story
films.note: Charles's comparison for the tone: 'Tom Hardy saw a Marriage Story and was essentially like, what if we replace all of the characters with shaken-up bottles of Mountain Dew.'

films.title: Bride of Frankenstein
films.note: Van's touchstone for the film's operatic, gothic-romance streak. Andy Serkis independently cites the same movie later in the episode.

films.title: Logan
films.note: Van's example of another comic book sequel that lowered the stakes instead of raising them, James Mangold's Wolverine send-off.

films.title: Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Rings
films.note: The box-office comparison point (Venom's Thursday night take beat it), and Charles's example of a movie he thinks would've worked better as a straight martial-arts film without the CGI dragon finale.

films.title: Spider-Man: No Way Home
films.note: The post-credits stinger pulls Eddie and Venom into a version of the Spider-Man universe via J. Jonah Jameson's broadcast; the three debate whether Venom belongs there.

films.title: Blue Valentine
films.note: Van's reference point for Michelle Williams, cast against type opposite a CGI symbiote.

films.title: Morbius
films.note: Brought up via its trailer, which shows Michael Keaton's Vulture, suggesting Sony is building a Spider-Man-adjacent universe outside the MCU.

films.title: Black Panther: Wakanda Forever
films.note: Referred to mid-conversation as 'Black Panther 2.' The group discusses Namor's casting and his history as an ally and enemy of the Avengers, X-Men, and Fantastic Four alike.

films.title: Kraven the Hunter
films.note: Name-checked (as 'Craven the Hunter') with Aaron Taylor-Johnson attached, part of the wider talk about Sony building its own Spider-Man-adjacent villain slate.

segments.heading: Sean's Top Five Comic Book Movie Sequels
segments.subject: Comic Book Movie Sequels

films.title: The Dark Knight
films.note: No. 1. Shared with Charles. Sean's case: the best movie featuring a superhero ever made, superhero movie or not.

films.title: Batman Returns
films.note: No. 2. Shared with Van, at his No. 5. Tim Burton making his own comic book movie without studio interference: Max Shreck, the Penguin, and Michelle Pfeiffer's Catwoman.

films.title: Avengers: Infinity War
films.note: No. 3. Shared with Charles, at his No. 5. You can't have Endgame without it, and it's the movie that put the whole Marvel universe onscreen at once without any of it looking out of place.

films.title: X2
films.note: No. 4. A rare sequel better than its predecessor, with Stryker as a sharper villain than Magneto and a mutants-vs-humanity theme that lands harder than the first film's.

films.title: Logan
films.note: No. 5. The only pick on Sean's list that plays as a genuine ending, a story willing to let go of the character.

segments.heading: Charles's Top Five Comic Book Movie Sequels
segments.subject: Comic Book Movie Sequels

films.title: The Dark Knight
films.note: No. 1. Shared with Sean.

films.title: Captain America: The Winter Soldier
films.note: No. 2. Charles's favorite MCU entry, one he recommends to people as a standalone spy thriller with zero other Marvel homework required.

films.title: Thor: Ragnarok
films.note: No. 3. The Jack Kirby energy Charles has wanted from a Marvel movie for years, delivered via Taika Waititi.

films.title: Spider-Man 2
films.note: No. 4, tied with 'X2.' Half of Charles's favorite superhero-sequel double feature, the genre 'perfected' by actors who'd grown into their roles.

films.title: X2
films.note: No. 4, tied with 'Spider-Man 2.' The other half of the double feature; expands Wolverine and grounds the mutants-vs-humanity theme through Stryker.

films.title: Avengers: Infinity War
films.note: No. 5. Made Charles cry in the theater, 'a perfect, perfect comic book movie' where every character shares the screen without looking out of place.

segments.heading: Van's Top Five Comic Book Movie Sequels
segments.note: Best to worst. Van's No. 4 pick got lost in the crosstalk and never made it onto tape.
segments.subject: Comic Book Movie Sequels

films.title: Captain America: The Winter Soldier
films.note: No. 1. Van's case: a bigger superhero sequel than 'The Dark Knight' because it expanded Captain America as a character and reshaped the MCU, introducing the Russo brothers and killing off S.H.I.E.L.D., even though he agrees 'Dark Knight' is the better movie.

films.title: The Dark Knight
films.note: No. 2. Van's knock: a great psychological thriller with a man in a bat costume in it, not really a superhero movie.

films.title: Avengers: Endgame
films.note: No. 3. The theater experience clinches it: 'the arclight was rocking' the night he saw it.

films.title: Batman Returns
films.note: No. 5. A pick 'from the heart' so Tim Burton's Batman run isn't left out; Van credits it as the template for the modern superhero sequel.

films.title: Venom: Let There Be Carnage
films.note: Devised by Tom Hardy, who brought Serkis in after working with him on performance capture; written by Hardy's close friend Kelly Marcel. Serkis built distinct physicalities for Venom (heavyweight, 'a gorilla crossed with a killer whale') and Carnage (serpentine, developed with dancers and parkour artists), shot by Bob Richardson in a fuller frame than the first film, chasing an '80s thriller look with a dash of gothic romance.

films.title: Border
films.note: Serkis's answer to the show's closing 'last great thing you've seen' question: Ali Abbasi's genre-bending Swedish film, written by the author of 'Let the Right One In.' He calls it unpredictable, 'beautiful and terrifying.'

Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Ring.title: Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Rings
